text: Gas separations using porous materials are of great economic importance. Designing adsorbents that have the desired properties for high selectivity is a materials design challenge that is not well understood. MOFs offer the possibility of almost unlimited architectural design for functional materials. Based on the enormous possibilities of MOF design, quantum mechanical calculations can serve as a guide to focus materials design on structures that will give the desired properties before they are synthesized. Here, we use computational chemistry methods to demonstrate the design of a MOF material that has the capability of separating dinitrogen (N2) from methane (CH4) — one of the hardest separations to achieve in particular for natural gas processing. By incorporating open V(II) sites in the MOF structure, an increased enthalpy for N2 adsorption compared to CH4 is achieved. This selectivity of the two gases is a result of a specific back-bonding of the V(II) centres with N2, which is not present for CH4.
